title: Anger at a Mentor Who Took Credit
dek: *When someone you trusted to guide you instead erases your contribution, the anger that rises isn't pettiness or ego. It's your nervous system recognizing a rupture in reciprocity, a violation of the implicit contract that mentorship involves seeing and naming what the student brings. This piece walks through what happens in your body when credit is stolen, why the mentor-student context makes the wound distinct, and how to metabolize the anger without either swallowing it or burning the bridge before you're ready.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Anger at a mentor who took credit reveals a boundary violation that your body registered before your mind could name it. This specific betrayal carries dual wounds: the theft itself and the silencing expectation that you should feel grateful anyway. Working with this anger means honoring both the relationship you thought you had and the one that actually existed.

You've been told that anger at a mentor is ungrateful. That framing keeps you stuck. It pathologizes a legitimate response to a real injury. When someone in a position of authority takes credit for your work, your anger isn't a character flaw. It's information. In our experience, this particular betrayal lands differently than other workplace credit theft because mentorship carries an implicit promise: I will help you become visible, not render you invisible in service of my own profile. The anger you feel when that promise breaks isn't about a single moment of attribution failure. It's about the sudden realization that the relationship you thought you were in - one of mutual regard, where your growth mattered - may have been something else entirely. Maybe you were always a resource to be mined. Maybe your mentor never saw you as a future peer but as a permanent subordinate whose ideas existed to burnish their legacy. That recognition hits the body before it hits the mind. You might notice a hot pressure behind your sternum, a jaw that won't unclench, a startle response that fires when you see their name in your inbox. These aren't symptoms of poor anger management. They're your system's appropriate response to discovering that someone you trusted to see you clearly chose not to. ## The Dual Wound: Theft and the Expectation of Gratitude Soraya Chemaly writes about how women, in particular, are socialized to perform gratitude even when they're being exploited. This expectation doesn't disappear in mentorship - it intensifies. You're supposed to feel lucky someone important gave you attention at all. When that someone then takes credit for the work you did under their guidance, you face two injuries at once. The first is the theft itself. Your idea, your analysis, your months of labor - presented as theirs. Maybe they mentioned you in a footnote if you're fortunate. Maybe they didn't mention you at all. The intellectual property violation is real, and it has material consequences: funding you won't receive, opportunities that won't come, a professional narrative that now centers them instead of you. The second wound is subtler and often more corrosive. It's the ambient expectation that you should be grateful anyway. That you should focus on what you learned, not what was taken. That pointing out the theft makes you seem petty, ungracious, unable to see the big picture. This expectation doesn't come only from the mentor. It comes from colleagues who benefit from keeping power structures intact, from your own internalized messages about deference, from a professional culture that treats credit as something senior people naturally absorb. When you feel anger at both the act and the silencing of your response to the act, you're not overreacting. You're tracking two separate violations. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return often to the idea that anger is diagnostic. It tells you where a boundary has been crossed. In this case, two boundaries: the one around your work and the one around your right to name what happened. The anger might show up as rumination. You replay the moment you saw your work presented as theirs. You draft emails you don't send. You lose time scrolling through their recent accolades, feeling your chest tighten with each new recognition they receive. This isn't wasted energy - it's your psyche trying to integrate a reality that doesn't match the story you were living inside. ## What Happens in the Body When Credit Is Stolen Your nervous system doesn't distinguish between physical threats and social ones, especially when the social threat involves your livelihood and identity. When you discover that a mentor took credit, your body often responds as if you're facing a predator. Heart rate climbs. Digestion slows or stops. Blood flow prioritizes large muscle groups in case you need to fight or flee. But here's the trap: you can't fight your mentor the way you'd fight a peer, and you can't flee if you're still embedded in the same institution or field. The social power differential means that openly confronting them carries risks a peer conflict wouldn't. You might lose access to their network. You might be labeled difficult. You might find that others in your field - people who depend on this mentor's goodwill - distance themselves from you. So the activation stays in your body with nowhere to go. PubMed research on anger expression and cardiovascular load shows that suppressed anger doesn't disappear - it converts into chronic physiological stress. Your blood pressure stays raised. Your cortisol patterns flatten. Sleep becomes difficult because your system won't downregulate when it senses an unresolved threat. You might notice tension headaches that start at the base of your skull. A jaw so tight you wake with tooth pain. A startle response that fires at benign triggers - a notification sound, a colleague's name, the time of day when you usually checked email from your mentor. These aren't signs that you're fragile. They're signs that your body is trying to complete a defensive response it can't complete through direct action. Peter Levine's work on trauma and the body, detailed in [In an Unspoken Voice - Peter Levine](https://www.amazon.com/dp/158394594X?tag=spankyspinola-20), explores how incomplete defensive responses stay lodged in the nervous system. When you can't fight and can't flee, the energy mobilized for those actions remains bound in your tissues. The anger at your mentor isn't just a cognitive grievance - it's a physiological process seeking resolution. In my own practice, I've watched people describe this sensation as "carrying a stone in my chest" or "feeling like I'm vibrating at the wrong frequency." One person said it felt like their body was a clenched fist that wouldn't open. These descriptions point to the same phenomenon: anger that has nowhere to go becomes anger that lives in you. ## Why Mentor Betrayal Hits Differently Than Peer Theft When a peer takes credit, the violation is clear and the response options are relatively straightforward. You can confront them, loop in a supervisor, document the theft, distance yourself. The relationship is lateral. You don't owe them deference, and they don't hold structural power over your future. Mentor betrayal operates in a different register. Mentorship is supposed to be generative. The mentor is meant to use their position to strengthen your work, not absorb it. When they do the opposite, they violate not just professional ethics but a deeper relational contract. You trusted them with your unfinished thinking, your half-formed ideas, the vulnerable edge of your competence. You assumed that trust would be honored. Karla McLaren writes about anger as the boundary-setting emotion, the one that tells you when something precious has been compromised. In mentor relationships, what's precious isn't just the work itself - it's the belief that someone with power and experience sees you as a person worthy of investment rather than extraction. When that belief shatters, the anger carries grief inside it. You might find yourself cycling between rage and a kind of bewildered sadness. The rage says, "How dare they." The sadness says, "I thought they cared about me." Both are true. Both deserve space. The anger isn't negating the sadness, and the sadness isn't softening the legitimate fury at being used. This dual emotional current often confuses people. They think they should feel one clear thing - either angry enough to burn the bridge or sad enough to forgive. But mentorship betrayal doesn't resolve into a single emotion. It's a tangle, and trying to force it into simplicity before you've fully felt both strands usually means stuffing one of them down. The power differential also means your mentor likely won't experience consequences the way a peer would. They have institutional protection, established credibility, a network that will believe their version of events. You, meanwhile, are still building those things. The asymmetry isn't just about age or experience - it's about who gets to define reality when stories conflict. ## The Myth of "Rising Above It" Someone will tell you to rise above it. To focus on your own path. To see this as a learning experience about choosing better mentors next time. This advice, however well-intentioned, often functions as another silencing mechanism. "Rising above" anger doesn't mean transcending it through spiritual bypass. It means moving through it, which requires first moving into it. You can't process what you won't feel. And the cultural script that treats anger as something to overcome rather than something to inhabit keeps you from accessing the clarity anger provides. Harriet Lerner's work on anger emphasizes that it's not the feeling itself that's dangerous - it's what we do when we're afraid of the feeling. When you try to rise above anger before you've let it speak, you usually just drive it underground. It doesn't go away. It becomes ambient irritability, passive-aggressive remarks, a vague resentment that colors unrelated interactions. Over the years I've seen people try to spiritually bypass this anger by framing the theft as a "lesson" or a "growth opportunity" before they've actually reckoned with the harm. That reframing can be useful later, once you've honored the injury. But deployed too early, it's just another way of telling yourself your anger isn't legitimate. The anger at your mentor wants something from you. It wants you to stop pretending the relationship was something it wasn't. It wants you to acknowledge that you were harmed, that the harm wasn't your fault, that you didn't "manifest" this betrayal through poor boundary-setting or naive trust. It wants you to see clearly what happened so you can make different choices going forward - not from a place of cynicism, but from a place of self-protection that doesn't require you to harden entirely. Rising above, in the useful sense, means integrating what the anger teaches you without letting it calcify into bitterness. But you can't integrate what you haven't first welcomed. ## A Five-Minute Practice: Tracking the Anger in Your Body Find a place where you won't be interrupted. Sit or lie down in a position that feels stable. Close your eyes if that's comfortable, or soften your gaze toward the floor. Bring to mind a specific moment when you realized your mentor had taken credit. Don't try to tell yourself the whole story - just locate one image, one sentence, one scene. Notice what happens in your body. Don't try to change it. Just track it. Where does the sensation live? Your throat? Your chest? Your hands? Does it have a temperature? A texture? Does it move or stay still? Breathe into that place without trying to make the sensation go away. Imagine your breath is curious, not corrective. You're not trying to calm the anger - you're trying to be with it. Stay for ten breaths. If your mind wanders into narrative (what you should have said, what you'll do next), gently bring your attention back to the body. The story can wait. Right now, you're just learning the shape of the anger in your tissue. When you're ready, open your eyes. Notice what you notice. You don't need to do anything with this information yet. You're building a relationship with your anger, and that relationship starts with presence, not strategy. ## Naming What You've Lost (And What You Haven't) Part of metabolizing this anger is getting specific about what the theft cost you. Not in a catastrophizing way, but in a clear-eyed inventory. Did you lose a publication opportunity? A grant? A speaking invitation? Professional visibility that would have opened other doors? Name those losses. Write them down if that helps. The anger often feels overwhelming because it's trying to hold all the consequences at once. When you separate them into discrete items, the emotional charge often becomes more manageable. You're not dealing with one monolithic betrayal - you're dealing with several specific harms, each of which might require a different response. But also name what you haven't lost. You still have the knowledge you gained doing the work. You still have the skills you built. You still have your capacity to generate ideas, to see patterns, to contribute. Your mentor took credit, but they didn't take your competence. They can't. That lives in you. This isn't minimizing the harm. It's refusing to let the theft colonize more territory than it actually occupies. When anger is huge and diffuse, it's easy to feel like everything is ruined. When you get specific, you often find that some things are intact. That distinction matters. It gives you ground to stand on while you decide what to do next. You also haven't lost your right to tell the truth about what happened. You might choose not to, for strategic reasons, but the option remains. Some people find relief in naming the theft to trusted colleagues, not as gossip but as reality-testing. "This happened. I'm not imagining it, right?" That external validation can help when you're doubting your own perception. [The assessments hub](/assessments) offers several tools for tracking emotional clarity when you're in the middle of a relational rupture. Sometimes seeing your experience reflected in a framework helps you trust that what you're feeling is proportional to what happened. ## When (and How) to Confront Confrontation isn't always wise, but sometimes it's necessary. The question isn't whether your mentor "deserves" to be confronted - it's whether confronting them serves your well-being and your goals. If you're still financially or professionally dependent on this person, direct confrontation might carry costs you're not ready to bear. That's not cowardice - it's pragmatism. You get to protect yourself first. Anger doesn't demand immediate expression. It demands acknowledgment and, eventually, some form of resolution. Resolution doesn't always mean confrontation. But if you've decided to confront, here's what helps: be specific, be clear, and expect nothing. Don't go in hoping they'll apologize or make it right. Go in because you need to say the thing out loud, to their face, and then you need to move forward whether they respond well or poorly. Use "I" statements not because they're morally superior but because they're harder to argue with. "I noticed my work on X was presented as yours in Y context. That's not accurate, and it's not okay." You're not asking for their interpretation. You're stating a fact. Keep it short. Don't justify your anger or explain why you're bringing it up. The more you explain, the more you invite them to debate your feelings. You don't need their agreement that you have a right to be upset. You're not asking for permission. If they deflect, deny, or minimize, don't try to convince them. You've said what you came to say. What they do with it is their choice. Your job is to stop carrying the unsaid thing in your body. Some people find it useful to bring a witness - another colleague, a union rep, someone whose presence changes the power dynamic slightly. Others prefer to do it in writing, which creates a record and gives them time to choose their words carefully. There's no single right way. The right way is the one that lets you feel like you honored your own boundary. ## Rebuilding Trust in Mentorship (Slowly) This betrayal will change how you approach mentorship going forward. That's not damage - it's learning. You're not broken because you're now more cautious. You're informed. Some people respond by refusing future mentorship entirely, which is understandable but often limits their growth. Others swing to the opposite extreme, performing hyper-independence to prove they don't need anyone's help. Both responses are protective, and both come with costs. A middle path: you can stay open to mentorship while building in structural protections. Document your contributions in real time. Use shared files with timestamps. Send summary emails after meetings where ideas are discussed. These practices aren't cynical - they're clear-eyed. You're not assuming the worst about everyone. You're acknowledging that power dynamics create vulnerability, and you're reducing that vulnerability where you can. You might also find yourself drawn to peer mentorship rather than hierarchical models. Working with people at your level or slightly ahead of you, where the power differential is smaller and the risk of exploitation lower. That's legitimate. Mentorship doesn't have to mean a senior person bestowing wisdom on a junior one. It can mean mutual support among people navigating similar terrain. If you do enter another formal mentorship, pay attention to how the person talks about their previous students. Do they name them? Credit them? Speak of them with respect? Or do they treat past mentees as extensions of their own work, tools that helped them build their legacy? The latter is a red flag. [The fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) includes resources for setting boundaries in unequal relationships without seeming defensive or closed. Sometimes the work isn't learning to trust again - it's learning to trust differently, with more information and less naiveté. ## Somatic Support for Anger That Won't Discharge When the anger stays high and your system won't settle, sometimes you need to support the body directly while the emotional processing unfolds at its own pace. Movement helps. Not gentle yoga (though that has its place) - something more vigorous. Running, boxing, dancing hard enough to sweat. You're giving your body a way to complete the defensive action it couldn't take in the moment of betrayal. The anger mobilized energy for a fight that didn't happen. Moving intensely lets some of that energy discharge. Some people find that [THORNE - 5-HTP - Contains Vitamin B6 - Serotonin Support - 90 Capsules](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00GDXHBS2?tag=spankyspinola-20) helps when the anger has created a chronic low-serotonin state - when you're irritable all the time, sleep is disrupted, and nothing feels satisfying. This isn't about suppressing the anger. It's about supporting your neurochemistry so the anger doesn't tip into depression. At night, if your mind won't stop replaying the betrayal, you might try [Yogi Soothing Caramel Bedtime Tea (64 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B083STP4NN?tag=spankyspinola-20) as part of a wind-down routine. The ritual matters as much as the ingredients. You're telling your body that the day's vigilance can pause, that you don't have to solve this tonight. [Our herbal cabinet](/herbs) has entries on bitters for digestive support when anger has shut down your gut, and on nervines for when the anger has frayed your stress tolerance to the point where small annoyances feel enormous. Somatic support doesn't fix the relational injury, but it can keep your body from bearing the entire load while you work through the emotional complexity. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### What if confronting my mentor would hurt my career? Then don't confront them, at least not yet. Your anger is valid whether or not you express it directly to the person who caused it. You can honor the anger, process it, let it inform your boundaries, and still choose not to confront if the professional cost is too high. Self-protection isn't the same as avoidance. Sometimes the wisest thing is to create distance, document what happened for your own records, and invest your energy in relationships that are reciprocal. You're not obligated to sacrifice your livelihood to prove your anger is real. ### How do I know if I'm overreacting? If multiple people in your life who know the details say you're overreacting, it's worth considering whether your response is proportional. But if you're asking this question in isolation, or if the only people telling you to let it go are people who benefit from you staying quiet, you're probably not overreacting. Theft of intellectual labor is a real harm with real consequences. Anger is the proportional response. The fact that the culture tries to minimize this harm doesn't mean your anger is excessive - it means the culture protects people in power at the expense of people without it. ### Should I warn others about this mentor? This is a personal decision with no universal right answer. Some people feel a responsibility to warn others, especially if the mentor has a pattern of taking credit. Others decide that the professional risk of being labeled a troublemaker isn't worth it. If you do choose to warn someone, do it privately, stick to facts rather than interpretations, and let them draw their own conclusions. "When I worked with X, they presented my work as theirs in these specific instances" is different from "X is a credit thief and you shouldn't work with them." The first is information. The second is a judgment that might get back to the mentor and create blowback. ## The Anger That Teaches Discernment Anger at a mentor who took credit doesn't just tell you about that mentor. It tells you about yourself - what you will and won't accept, what you're willing to risk to name a wrong, where your loyalty to truth outweighs your loyalty to comfort. This anger, if you let it, will sharpen your discernment. Not in a cynical way that assumes everyone is out to exploit you, but in a grounded way that notices red flags earlier, that trusts your gut when something feels off, that doesn't override your own perception to maintain a relationship that isn't actually serving you. The mentor who took credit taught you something, though not what they were supposed to teach. They taught you that titles and reputation don't guarantee integrity. That someone can be brilliant in their field and still capable of casual cruelty. That the person who praises your potential in private might erase your contribution in public if it serves them. You don't have to be grateful for those lessons. You didn't sign up for them. But you can let them inform how you move through professional spaces without letting them close you off entirely. The anger, held well, becomes a kind of inner compass. It's not telling you to trust no one. It's telling you to trust yourself first. Some of the work we explore at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) looks at how betrayal by a trusted figure can actually be an initiation - not because the betrayal was good or necessary, but because your response to it shapes who you become. You can let this break you into bitterness, or you can let it forge you into someone who sees clearly and sets boundaries from that clarity. The anger doesn't make that choice for you. It just holds the space while you decide. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on anger at a mentor who took credit, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
